content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 13, 2017

Douglas L. White
Churchwell White LLP
1414 K Street, 3rd Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-015

Dear Mr. White:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Miguel Community Services District Director Joseph Parent regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹

Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Luis Obispo County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act prohibit Director Parent from taking part in governmental decisions relating to collective bargaining agreements covering the District's employees given that the Director's wife is one of those employees?

2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Director from making or participating in the making of, or the District from entering into, the collective bargaining agreements?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-015
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. The Act does not prohibit the Director from taking part in decisions relating to the
collective bargaining agreement so long as the decisions apply equally to all District employees in
the same bargaining unit or representative group as the Director's wife.

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Director from making or participating in the
making of, or the District from entering into, the collective bargaining agreements because Section
1091.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies.

FACTS

You are the General Counsel of the San Miguel Community Services District and the
authorized representative of Joseph Parent, a member of the District's Board of Directors. Director
Parent was elected to a four-year term, and was sworn into office on December 22, 2016.

The District has employed the Director's wife for a number of years, and for longer than a
year before the Director's election to the Board. The Director's wife currently serves as an
"Operations Coordinator/Account Clerk 2" for the District. In this role, she handles, among other
things, the posting of meeting agendas and receipt of customer payments to the District.

The District will soon consider collective bargaining agreements governing the employment
of the District's five employees, including the Director's wife. In considering the collective
bargaining agreements, the District may review its personnel rules, drug and alcohol policies, and
disciplining rules applicable to all District employees.

You provided additional facts on March 6, 2017.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Under Section 87103, an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and
those of immediate family members. Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as the spouse and
dependent children. Therefore, the Director has an interest in his personal finances and those of his
wife with respect to the decisions at issue.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official's agency. (Regulation

File No. A-17-015
Page No. 3

18701(a).) An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the official's interest. (Ibid.)

The Director's interest in his personal finances is the subject of the decisions relating to the collective bargaining agreements because those decisions involve his wife's entitlements and contractual obligations as a District employee. Therefore, the effect of the decisions on the Director's interest in his personal finances is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18702.5(a) provides that a "personal financial effect" means the effect of a decision on the personal finances of an official or his or her immediate family member, and that such an effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. However, Regulation 18702.5(b)(1) provides that a "personal financial effect" does not include any establishment of, or change to, benefits provided under an employment or retirement policy for employees or retirees if the decision's effect applies equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group.

The District will soon consider collective bargaining agreements covering all five of the District's employees, including the Director's wife. If the decisions regarding the agreements would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss with respect to the Director's personal finances, the personal financial effect of those decisions would be material. However, if the decisions' effect applies equally to all District employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group, it would not be material. Therefore, the Act does not prohibit the Director from taking part in the decisions regarding the agreements so long as those decisions apply equally to all District employees in the same bargaining unit or representative group as his wife.²

Section 1090

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate and distinct from that applied under the Act.

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a public officer or employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply?

________________________________
² You have also asked whether the Act would prohibit the Director from taking part in decisions before the District's Finance and Budget Subcommittee. The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply to particular decisions. We are unable to apply the Act's conflict of interest provisions to your question because it does not identify a particular decision before the Subcommittee. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(C) and (D).) If you identify a particular decision before the Subcommittee that may give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act in the future, you should seek further advice.

File No. A-17-015
Page No. 4

Steps one through four are not at issue here. This matter involves a public officer, the
Director, and a contract, the collective bargaining agreements. The Director would be making or
participating in making of the agreements because an officer is conclusively presumed to be
involved in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the officer is a member of a board or
commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, p. 649.) And the
Director has a financial interest in the agreements because they will govern his wife's employment
by the District. Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the Director from making or participating in the
making of, and the City from entering into, the agreements, unless a remote or noninterest
exception or the rule of necessity applies.

Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply?

The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition. (See
Sections 1091 and 1091.5) Section 1091.5 sets forth noninterest exceptions to the prohibition. If a
noninterest is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. (City of Vernon v.
Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158,
159-160 (2001).)

Section 1091.5(a)(6) provides that an officer of a public agency shall not be deemed to be
interested in a contract if the officer's interest in the contract is the officer's spouse's employment
by the agency, and the spouse's employment has existed for at least one year prior to the officer's
election or appointment.

The Director's interest in the collective bargaining agreements is in his wife's employment
by the District. And the District has employed the Director's wife for a number of years, and for
longer than a year before the Director's election to the Board. Therefore, the Director has a
noninterest in the agreements pursuant to Section 1091.5(a)(6).

Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Director from making or participating in
the making of, or the District from entering into, the agreements because Section 1091.5(a)(6)'s
noninterest exception applies. Accordingly, our Section1090 analysis ends here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Director from making or participating in the making of, or 
the District from entering into, the collective bargaining agreements?

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Director from making or participating in the

l 091.5( a)( 6) 's non interest exception applies.
